summary: Gruber dissects the updated iTunes Store Terms of Service accompanying Apple's new movie rental feature, running a diff on the old and new versions to identify significant changes. He finds most changes unremarkable but zeroes in on two notable limitations: HD movies being restricted to Apple TV only (likely due to HDCP piracy concerns), and the 24-hour viewing window after pressing play. While he praises the overall rental offering — pricing, quality options, and Apple TV shopping UI — he argues the 24-hour window is unnecessarily stingy and out of step with the flexibility of traditional VHS and DVD rentals.
key_points: HD rental movies are restricted to Apple TV only, likely because it supports HDCP DRM output, not because of file size concerns
Movies rented on Apple TV cannot be moved to other devices, while Mac-rented movies can be transferred
The 24-hour viewing window after first pressing play is far too restrictive compared to the generous 30-day download window
The viewing window limitation has no plausible piracy justification since VHS and DVD rentals always allowed multiple viewings over several days
Despite the viewing window complaint, Gruber considers the overall rental offering strong with only this one real sour spot
money_quotes: The 30-day period is fine, but the 24-hour viewing window is far too miserly.

Everything else about these movie rentals seems terrific — the DVD and HD quality options, the prices, the shopping UI on the Apple TV — but 24 hours after you hit play the first time just doesn't seem fair.
You go to bed, and now you're sort of stuck: you've got to finish the movie the next night before 10 pm or your carriage turns back into a pumpkin.
I just don't get why it's so miserly. What skin would it be off the studios' or Apple's back if they gave you 48 or 72 hours?
Who cares if a two- or three-day window would allow you to watch the entire movie multiple times? That's something you've always been able to do with VHS and DVD rentals.
I don't think it's a deal-breaker, but this 24-hour window is the only real sour spot in the whole deal.
key_insight: Apple's iTunes movie rental service gets nearly everything right, but the 24-hour viewing window is an unjustifiably stingy limitation that has no real piracy rationale and punishes normal viewing habits.
